note: construct = {dist, std_or_min, max}, matching FirstFloorElevationUncertainty(IDistributionEnum distributionEnum, double standardDeviationOrMinimum, double maximum = double.MaxValue). `sample` asserts FirstFloorElevationUncertainty.Sample(probability) DIRECTLY -- the RAW offset, with NO post-processing (the C# test theory applies inv+offset additively, or inv*offset for LogNormal, but this fixture checks the pre-multiplication/pre-addition offset itself). Cross-check against the C# theory: row 1 (Normal) offset should satisfy 5 + offset = 4.873326 => offset ~= -0.126674; row 5 (Deterministic) offset = 0 (10 + 0 = 10). Unlike ValueUncertainty/ValueRatioWithUncertainty, there is NO negative clamp -- offsets may be negative. `sample_iteration` asserts Sample(iteration, computeIsDeterministic) with args [iteration, computeIsDeterministic]; the two cases below use computeIsDeterministic=true: LogNormal always returns 1, everything else returns 0 (centerOfDistribution).
